content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

July 3, 1997


Kim David Stafford
Councilmember
City of Vallejo
555 Santa Clara Street
Post Office Box 3068
Vallejo, California 95804

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-177

Dear Mr. Stafford:

This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Under the Act, may you participate in the city council decision to approve dockside
improvements to the ferry building and floats owned by the Mare Island Ferry Company
since the owner of the company made a loan/contribution to your campaign committee?

CONCLUSION

You may participate in the city council decision regarding improvements to the ferry
building and floats. The contribution from the owner of the Mare Island Ferry Company
does not constitute a conflict of interest.

FACTS

You are a councilmember in the City of Vallejo. On August 8, 1995, the Campaign
to Elect Kim David Stafford was given a $2,200 loan with no interest by Victor Raahauge,
owner of the Mare Island Ferry Company dba The Wharf. On September 30, 1996, the final
repayment was made and the loan was paid in full. The loan was repaid from your campaign
bank account and reported accordingly.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-177
Page 2

In December 1996, you were appointed by the city council to sit on the transportation
committee, an advisory committee that makes recommendations to the city council. The
committee is composed solely of three city councilmembers.

A project before the transportation committee concerns use of the ferry building and
floats owned by the Mare Island Ferry Company. On July 24, 1997, the council will be
voting on whether to approve dockside improvements to the ferry building and floats owned
by the Mare Island Ferry Company.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 87103(c) specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on:

"(c) Any source of income, other than gifts and other than loans by a
commercial lending institution in the regular course of business on terms
available to the public without regard to official status, aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by or
promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made."

Thus, you are prohibited from making or in any way participating in any decision
that will have a foreseeable material financial effect on a source of income to you. You have
inquired whether a loan to your campaign committee from the owner of the Mare Island
Ferry Company constitutes income and triggers a conflict of interest.

On December 8, 1995, Victor Raahauge, the owner of the Mare Island Ferry Co.,
made a loan to your campaign committee, which was reported as such on Form 490,
Schedule B. On September 30, 1996, your committee made the final repayment on the loan,
which was also reported on Form 490, Schedule B. Pursuant to Section 84216, "a loan
received by a candidate or committee is a contribution unless the loan is received from a
commercial lending institution in the ordinary course of business, or it is clear from the
surrounding circumstances that it is not made for political purposes." Section 82030, which
defines income, specifically excludes campaign contributions (Section 82030(b)(1)). Since
the loan was a contribution and not income, it is not an economic interest for purposes of the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.

File No. A-97-177
Page 3

We must also examine if there is a conflict of interest under Section 84308, which
applies to members of appointed boards and commissions. Section 84308 exempts local
governmental agencies whose members are directly elected by the voters. However, as a
councilmember, you were appointed to the transportation committee, which advises the city
council. Regulation 18438.1(a) (copy enclosed), provides that "the officers of an agency
exempted by Government Code Section 84308(a)(3) are exempted only when:

"(2) They are acting as members of any committee or subgroup of the
governing body of the agency which is composed solely of members of the
governing body of the agency."

You are exempt because you are a member of an appointed committee that is
composed solely of city councilmembers. (Regulation 18438.1(a)(2), above.) Therefore,
the contribution from the owner of the Mare Island Ferry Company also does not constitute a
conflict of interest under Section 84308.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Jil Stepher
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JS:ak

Enclosure

QUESTION: Under the Act, may you participate in the city council decision to approve dockside
improvements to the ferry building and floats owned by the Mare Island Ferry Company
since the owner of the company made a loan/contribution to your campaign committee?

CONCLUSION: You may participate in the city council decision regarding improvements to the ferry
building and floats. The contribution from the owner of the Mare Island Ferry Company
does not constitute a conflict of interest.
